title: Python Security Patterns
description: Python security from basics to Staff+ FAANG depth: injection prevention, SAST, dependency scanning, secrets management, pickle risks, sandboxing, and supply chain security.

sections.title: 1. Injection Prevention — SQL, Command, and Template Injection
sections.description:
Python applications are vulnerable to injection when untrusted input is concatenated into SQL, shell commands, or templates. Use parameterized queries, shlex.quote(), and safe templates.

SQL injection: Never use f-strings for SQL. Use cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', [user_id]) with param substitution. For ORMs (SQLAlchemy), always use the ORM's parameterized interface.

Command injection: Never use os.system() or subprocess(shell=True) with user input. Use subprocess.run(['ls', '-l', filename]) with shell=False (default).

Template injection (SSTI): Never render user-controlled strings through Jinja2 with autoescape=False. Use autoescape=True and validate template names against an allowlist.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your team has 50 Python services. How do you ensure no SQL injection anywhere?' — Static analysis in CI (semgrep/bandit) + mandatory parameterized queries in code review + quarterly penetration testing.
sections.codeBlock:
# === SQL injection SAFE === def get_user_safe(user_id: int) -> dict | None: cur = conn.cursor() cur.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s', (user_id,)) return cur.fetchone() # === SQL injection UNSAFE === def get_user_unsafe(user_id: str) -> dict | None: cur = conn.cursor() cur.execute(f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}') # DANGEROUS return cur.fetchone() # === Command injection SAFE === import subprocess subprocess.run(['ls', '-l', user_provided_path], check=True) # shell=False is default # === Command injection UNSAFE === import os os.system(f'ls -l {user_provided_path}') # DANGEROUS: shell interprets # === Template injection safe === from jinja2 import Environment, select_autoescape env = Environment(autoescape=True) template = env.from_string('

{{ title }}

') print(template.render(title='')) # escaped # === shlex for safe shell argument quoting === import shlex safe_arg = shlex.quote(user_input) subprocess.run(['ssh', 'host', safe_arg], check=True)

sections.title: 2. SAST and Dependency Scanning
sections.description:
Static Application Security Testing (SAST) scans source code for vulnerabilities without running it. bandit is the standard Python SAST: bandit -r my_project/. It detects hardcoded passwords, SQL injection patterns, shell injection, eval usage, and more.

Dependency scanning: pip-audit checks installed packages against known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures). safety checks against a curated vulnerability database. Always run in CI as a blocking check.

SBOM (Software Bill of Materials): cyclonedx-bom generates an SPDX/CycloneDX SBOM for your project. Google requires SBOMs for all production services.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Our Python monorepo has 500+ dependencies. A critical CVE is announced. How do we find and fix it in 24 hours?' — (1) pip-audit on every service to list affected packages. (2) Automated PR creation via Dependabot/Renovate. (3) Staged rollout with monitoring. (4) Postmortem to improve detection speed.
sections.codeBlock:
# === bandit (SAST) ===
# $ pip install bandit
# $ bandit -r src/ -f json -o report.json

# Common findings bandit catches:
# B101: assert used (assertions disabled with -O)
# B102: exec used
# B105: hardcoded password
# B108: world-writable file
# B110: try/except/pass
# B201: flask debug=True
# B301: pickle
# B303: md5/sha1 used

# === pip-audit (dependency scanning) ===
# $ pip install pip-audit
# $ pip-audit
# $ pip-audit --fix  # auto-upgrade vulnerable packages

# === Safety ===
# $ pip install safety
# $ safety check --full-report

# === SBOM generation ===
# $ pip install cyclonedx-bom
# $ cyclonedx-py -e --format json > sbom.json

# === CI integration (GitHub Actions) ===
# .github/workflows/security.yml
# name: Security Scan
# on: [push, pull_request]
# jobs:
#   security:
#     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
#     steps:
#       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#       - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
#       - run: pip install bandit pip-audit
#       - run: bandit -r . -f json | tee bandit.json
#       - run: pip-audit | tee audit.txt

sections.title: 3. Secrets Management
sections.description:
Never hardcode secrets. Use environment variables, vault services (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted config files.

dotenv: .env files for local dev only — NEVER commit them (.env in .gitignore).

Vault: HashiCorp Vault with short-lived tokens. hvac Python client fetches secrets at startup and on rotation.

Secret detection: trufflehog and git-secrets scan git history for committed secrets. Run pre-commit hook to prevent committing secrets.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design the secrets strategy for a new Python microservice' — (1) No hardcoded secrets — ever. (2) Use Vault with auto-rotation. (3) Encrypt secrets at rest and in transit. (4) Audit all secret access. (5) Rotate keys before they expire. (6) Incident response plan for compromised secrets.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Environment variables (12-factor app) ===
import os
DATABASE_URL = os.environ['DATABASE_URL']  # required, fails fast if missing
LOG_LEVEL = os.environ.get('LOG_LEVEL', 'INFO')  # optional with default

# NEVER:
# DATABASE_URL = 'postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/db'

# === python-dotenv for local dev ===
# .env file (in .gitignore!)
# DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/dev
# from dotenv import load_dotenv
# load_dotenv()  # loads .env for local dev only

# === HashiCorp Vault ===
# import hvac
# client = hvac.Client(url='https://vault.example.com', token=os.environ['VAULT_TOKEN'])
# secret = client.secrets.kv.v2.read_secret_version(path='myapp/db')
# DATABASE_URL = secret['data']['data']['url']

# === AWS Secrets Manager ===
# import boto3
# from botocore.config import Config
# client = boto3.client('secretsmanager', config=Config(connect_timeout=5))
# resp = client.get_secret_value(SecretId='myapp/db')
# DATABASE_URL = json.loads(resp['SecretString'])['url']

# === Pre-commit secret detection ===
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
# repos:
#   - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
#     rev: v4.5.0
#     hooks:
#       - id: detect-private-key
#       - id: check-added-large-files
#   - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
#     rev: v1.4.0
#     hooks:
#       - id: detect-secrets

sections.title: 4. Pickle Risks and Safely Deserializing Data
sections.description:
pickle.loads() can execute arbitrary code during deserialization. Never unpickle untrusted data. This is the #1 Python security mistake in FAANG interviews.

Safe alternatives: JSON (json.loads), MessagePack (msgpack), Protocol Buffers (protobuf), or Avro. For internal ML models, use safetensors (Hugging Face) which avoids pickle entirely.

If you must use pickle: Only between trusted services on a private network. Sign the data with HMAC. Use pickle.Unpickler with a restricted find_class.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your ML training pipeline loads model artifacts from a shared filesystem. How do you prevent RCE via malicious pickles?' — (1) Switch to safetensors or ONNX for model weights. (2) If pickle is unavoidable, use a restricted unpickler that only allows a whitelist of known classes. (3) Sign all artifacts with a service account key. (4) Scan artifacts for anomalies before loading.

sections.title: 5. Sandboxing and Process Isolation
sections.description:
Python has no native sandbox. eval()/exec() with user input is dangerous even with restricted globals — there are known escapes using ().__class__.__bases__[0].__subclasses__().

Subprocess isolation: Run untrusted Python code in a separate process via subprocess with resource limits (CPU, memory, timeout). Use Docker or gVisor for stronger isolation.

PyPy sandbox: PyPy has a sandbox mode that intercepts all system calls. Slower but safer than CPython for untrusted code execution.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design a code execution service that runs user-submitted Python safely' — (1) gVisor or Firecracker micro-VM per execution. (2) Resource limits (CPU quota, memory limit, wall-clock timeout). (3) No network access. (4) Read-only filesystem. (5) Kill on any forbidden syscall. (6) Stateless — no persistence between executions.
sections.codeBlock:
# === subprocess isolation with resource limits ===
import subprocess
import resource

def run_untrusted(code: str, timeout=5, max_mem=100*1024*1024):
    def set_limits():
        resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_AS, (max_mem, max_mem))
        resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_CPU, (timeout, timeout))

    proc = subprocess.run(
        ['python3', '-c', code],
        capture_output=True,
        timeout=timeout,
        preexec_fn=set_limits,  # set limits IN the child process
    )
    return proc.stdout.decode(), proc.stderr.decode()

# === eval() is NOT safe, even with restricted globals ===
def eval_safe(expr: str) -> Any:
    # STILL UNSAFE — can escape via __subclasses__
    return eval(expr, {'__builtins__': {}}, {})  # DANGEROUS

# Known escape:
# eval_safe("().__class__.__bases__[0].__subclasses__()")
# Returns all subclasses of object — including file readers, os, etc.

# === Docker for strong isolation ===
# $ docker run --rm --read-only --network=none \
#   --memory=100m --cpus=1 --pids-limit=50 \
#   python:3.12-slim python3 -c "your code here"

sections.title: 6. Staff+ Security Architecture
sections.description:
At Staff+ level, security is a design constraint, not a checklist item.

Threat modeling: Use STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) per service. Document threats and mitigations in design docs.

Supply chain security: Pin dependency versions (pip freeze > requirements.txt), use hash checking (--require-hashes), mirror to internal registry, scan all dependencies in CI. SLSA framework (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) at level 2+ is expected at FAANG.

Incident response: Every security issue gets a severity (SEV-1 through SEV-4). SEV-1 (active data breach): <5min to acknowledge, <30min to mitigate. Postmortem within 72 hours.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design the security strategy for a Python-based ML training platform' — (1) Network isolation for training clusters (no public IPs). (2) Encrypted model artifacts at rest and in transit. (3) Signed container images with admission control. (4) Audit logging for all data access. (5) Penetration testing before each major launch. (6) Bug bounty program for external researchers.
sections.codeBlock:
# SLSA framework: build integrity
# Level 1: Build script exists
# Level 2: Build runs on a hosted CI; provenance generated
# Level 3: Hardened CI (ephemeral, isolated); provenance signed
# Level 4: Two-person review of all build changes; hermetic builds

# Dependency pinning with hash verification
# requirements.txt (example):
# flask==3.0.0 --hash=sha256:abc...
# $ pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.txt

# Supply chain verification script
import hashlib
import json

def verify_dependency_integrity(package, version, expected_hash):
    import importlib.metadata
    dist = importlib.metadata.distribution(package)
    # Verify installed package hash matches expected
    actual = hashlib.sha256(dist.read_text('RECORD').encode()).hexdigest()
    if actual != expected_hash:
        raise ValueError(f'Integrity check failed for {package} {version}')

# Pre-commit security hooks
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
# repos:
#   - repo: https://github.com/PyCQA/bandit
#     rev: 1.7.7
#     hooks:
#       - id: bandit
#         args: ['-r', 'src/', '-ll']

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• bandit — Python SAST tool
  • pip-audit — Dependency vulnerability scanner
  • Safety CLI — Python dependency security checker
  • PEP 506 — Python Security Model
  • OWASP Python Security Cheat Sheet
  • Black Hat Python (2nd ed.) — Chapters on Python exploitation and defense

details:
Security workflow: (1) Threat model every service (STRIDE). (2) Run SAST in CI (bandit). (3) Scan dependencies (pip-audit). (4) Never hardcode secrets. (5) Never unpickle untrusted data. (6) Pin dependencies with hash verification. (7) Incident response plan for each severity level.

Common mistakes: Hardcoding secrets 'just for testing'; using eval/exec with user input; unpickling model artifacts from network storage; running without SAST in CI; not pinning supply chain dependencies; SQL injection via f-strings.

Staff+ expectations: Design the security architecture for the service, not just the security features. Threat model at design time. Automate security scanning in CI. Write incident response playbooks. Mentor the team on secure coding patterns. Know that security is not a sprint — it's a continuous investment in process and culture.
